cover.title: THE SOIL
cover.subtitle: The one who makes every room easier, then forgets to stand somewhere too.
cover.tagline: You connect everyone else, but your own place keeps disappearing in the middle.

profile_scores.caption: High warmth, low pride. You adjust for everyone, but never quite make a place for yourself.

short_summary: You have a gift for making people feel connected, comfortable, and understood before they even ask.
But because you smooth everything over so quickly, almost no one notices when you're uncomfortable too.
What changes love for you is learning not to mediate every conflict before others learn how to meet you directly.

full_report.headline: THE SOIL
full_report.intro: You have a knack for connecting people. When things feel awkward, you naturally build the bridge, but in the middle of all that, your own position often disappears. This report follows the pattern underneath that: why you keep absorbing friction, what it costs you, and how to step out of the middle without losing love.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You have a knack for connecting people.

When two people feel awkward around each other, you naturally build a bridge. When someone's uncomfortable, you notice before they even say it. When a mood is about to break, you're the one who quietly steps in first and resets it.
So people say being around you, everyone ends up comfortable.
What goes unnoticed is your own discomfort.
You are quick to make everyone else comfortable. No one knows when you are uncomfortable.
Because you're the one who steps right in and smooths everything over, there's never any room left for your own position to even come up.
And something else can happen. When someone is struggling, their problem can start to feel like yours. No one asked you to take it on. You just notice the gap and move toward it.
So love starts the same way. At first, things between you and them, and between them and the people around you, all flow naturally, because you're managing both sides.
But in the middle of all that, you're not actually standing anywhere yourself.
chapters.profile_note: High Care, Low Pride. Adjusting for everyone, but never quite making a place for yourself

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: The early stage of a relationship is good.
You're managing both sides. Them and you, them and the people around you. So everything connects naturally.

But as time goes on, something strange happens. You slowly become the middle of the relationship. You smooth over the friction between them and other people, and you don't even let them deal with conflict between you and them. You step in and resolve it first, yourself.
One day, you look up and realize you've been absorbing most of the friction before anyone else has to face it.
You never chose that role outright. It grew each time you stepped in before the other person had to.
chapters.highlight: You're not exhausted because there's too much conflict. You're exhausted because every conflict has always disappeared by passing through you.

chart.title: THE GRAVITY CURVE
chart.description: The flow where you keep absorbing the relationship's friction, while your own conflicts pile up with nowhere to go.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.truth: You do like harmony. But awkwardness can pull you into action before you have time to ask what you actually want. Sometimes you're protecting the connection. Sometimes you're just trying to make the tension disappear.
items.truth_short: You like harmony, but awkwardness can make you step in before checking what you want.

items.truth: You can say no. It just gets harder when you can already see the gap your answer might leave. You're quick to notice what other people need from each other. The part you miss is what you need from them.
items.truth_short: You can say no, but you often notice everyone else's gap before your own.

items.belief: I always have to be the one keeping the balance in the middle.
items.truth: You don't always have to stay in the middle. Sometimes the honest thing is to stand on your own side and let that be visible.
items.truth_short: You don't always need to stay in the middle. Sometimes you need a position of your own.

items.truth: You are good at mediating. But being good at it can make stepping back feel wrong. You may move in because leaving the problem untouched feels harder than carrying it yourself.
items.truth_short: You're good at mediating, but stepping back can feel harder than taking the problem on.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You're particularly drawn to people who struggle with conflict and feel relieved when you step in.
At first it feels like you're helping by sorting things out from the middle.
But the longer the relationship goes on, you become the shock absorber for every conflict in it.
You can also be drawn to people who need extra support. Someone overwhelmed. Someone unsure of the next step. Someone who feels safer when you're nearby.
The problem is, even after you've fallen for them, you still can't put down the caretaker role.
chapters.highlight: Someone who finds you comfortable uses your mediation. Someone who loves you tries to work it out themselves, even without your stepping in.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't to get even better at mediating. You're already plenty good at it.
What you need is to occasionally not mediate.
The friction between them and other people. The conflict between you and them.
Letting both people stay in the conversation and work through it together can also be a form of love.
chapters.action_sentence: Letting both people stay in the conversation and work through it together can also be a form of love.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You already know how to connect people. You already know how to keep the balance. You already know how to make everyone comfortable.
What you're going to learn next is how to stay present without always stepping in.
Even when friction shows up. Even when it gets awkward.
Giving people room to work through their own part, while still letting your own position be heard.
chapters.transition_note: Giving people room to work through their own part, while still letting your own position be heard.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: Someone Who Keeps Handing You the Decision
items.description: They always ask you what to do. At first you assume it's because they trust you. But over time, they turn into someone who can't decide anything without you.

items.type_title: Someone Who Struggles to Name Their Feelings
items.description: They get tangled in what they're feeling, and you end up helping them name it. You may be the first one to ask, "Are you upset about this?"

items.type_title: Someone Who Steps Back When Problems Show Up
items.description: When something goes wrong, their share can quietly land with you. They say, "I don't know what to do," and wait for you to move first.
"Are we okay?" "You're not mad, are you?" "Do you still like me?" These questions may come often. Reassurance can help for a moment, but it cannot carry the whole relationship by itself.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: This often showed up early. You may have simply treated it as your role.

signal_strength.label: When conflict comes up, you're the one who steps in first to smooth it over
You're already managing even their other relationships, including family and friends
Even conflicts between you and them get a solution from you before they ever try to work it out themselves
Almost no one ever asks first what's bothering you

chapters.core_signal: Friction keeps passing through you until your effort starts to look like background noise instead of work.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: Conflict can happen without you stepping in first.
checklist.description: The relationship doesn't fall apart just because you let people work it out themselves.

checklist.title: Stating your own position first isn't strange.
checklist.description: The relationship is fine even when you stand on one side instead of standing in the middle.

checklist.title: The relationship doesn't break just because you don't mediate.
checklist.description: People can work through their own part without waiting for you to take over.

checklist.title: Your mediation isn't taken for granted.
checklist.description: This matters. Stepping into the middle should be appreciated, not treated as something that will always happen.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You're vulnerable to someone who avoids direct conflict and relies on you to make every awkward moment disappear. You keep standing in the middle, but when the conflict is between you and them, no one takes that role for you. Over time, the relationship can leave you doing all the emotional coordination alone.
sections.who_steadies_you: A steadier match is someone who tries to work things out directly, even when you don't step in first. With someone like this, you can have your own position without the whole relationship depending on you to restore the balance.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You may still be drawn to someone who feels safer when you take over the difficult parts. That attraction does not decide the ending. What matters is whether they learn to face discomfort with you, instead of leaving you to absorb it.
sections.next_signs: This time, the pattern may show up in smaller moments. A minor disagreement happens, and they wait for you to smooth it over instead of speaking directly. Notice whether that changes as the two of you get to know each other.
sections.try_this: When someone brings you a conflict, listen before offering a solution. Ask what they want to say or do next. Stay present, but leave enough room for them to take part in working it through.
sections.final_line: The real change is whether conflict can be shared, instead of always disappearing through you.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to connect people and smooth over conflict. What's left is learning when to let others meet the friction themselves.
action_items: This week, let one small awkward moment exist long enough for both people to speak.
When someone brings you conflict, listen first and ask what they want to do next.
Say your own position once before trying to balance everyone else's.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to stand between people. This report covered why you keep ending up in that position, and how you relearn what it means to step out of it.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
